other_side: The edition's lead is that CEOs who personally own AI strategy report roughly three times the ROI. Zitron's argument is that AI's return on investment cannot actually be measured at all — there is no standard way to price a task given hallucinations and an ever-shifting list of agentic harnesses — so "reported ROI" from a survey is sentiment, not arithmetic. He points to a Bain study of 951 executives whose own conclusion was, in his quote, "The technology worked. The value didn't arrive," and warns that firms self-funding the next wave from past savings are making "a circular bet with a structural leak." And he argues that making AI use a CEO-owned KPI is precisely what manufactures inflated self-reports: people conditioned to ignore the true, now-rising token cost keep claiming value while nobody can point to a measured dollar back.
truth_beneath: Both men orbit one variable: whether enterprise AI yields a real, measurable return once a firm pays its true, unsubsidised cost. Both accept that companies are spending heavily, that token-based pricing has now arrived, and that cost accounting is poor — this very edition notes only about a third of organisations can fully see their AI costs. The empirical question that will settle it is whether audited outcomes, not survey sentiment, show a positive net return; the observable that resolves it is hard productivity or profit data at real prices set against spend. The residue is pure worldview: Whittemore reads rising executive confidence and "reported ROI" as signal, Zitron reads the same self-reports as a con that dissolves the moment anyone measures. Whittemore is vindicated if owned, accountable AI shows durable audited gains; Zitron if "reported" value keeps failing to become a demonstrable dollar. Both are polemicists with incentives — NLW bullish and anti-doom, Zitron bubble-certain with a paid newsletter — and the signal is that both concede firms cannot yet measure the cost. Watch whether anyone publishes audited, priced-at-cost ROI once the subsidies end.
